planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 move outdoors to a shaded location March–May. Escargot performs best as a container plant that can be moved indoors when temperatures drop below 55°F — maintaining it as an indoor-outdoor plant allows the spiral foliage to be appreciated year-round. Rex begonias prefer high humidity (above 50%), making zone 9's humid summer climate well-suited to outdoor summer use in deep shade. Avoid wetting the leaves — water at the soil level only to prevent powdery mildew and leaf spotting. Do not overwater — allow the top inch of soil to dry between waterings.

directions: Plant in deep shade to bright indirect light in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic, consistently moist but never waterlogged soil — Rex begonias are moisture-sensitive in both directions, suffering from both drought and standing water; Escargot is one of the most famous and distinctive Rex begonia cultivars, producing leaves whose silvery-green surface is marked with a dramatic spiral pattern — the leaf base curls around itself in a tight coil before unfurling, creating a permanent snail-shell spiral pattern on each leaf that gives the variety its name. The silver-green leaf surface with darker green spiral accents and red-purple undersides creates a truly extraordinary visual effect unlike any other commonly grown plant. Rex begonias (Begonia rex-cultorum) are cultivated entirely for their extraordinarily varied and complex leaf patterns rather than their flowers — the small, insignificant flowers are typically removed to redirect energy into foliage production. Escargot is considered one of the most architectural and conversation-starting plants in the shade garden precisely because the spiral leaf pattern is so unexpected and perfect that it appears designed rather than grown. In zone 9 it excels as an outdoor container plant for deep shade areas during the warm months, then transitions easily indoors as a houseplant for cooler periods.
sources: American Begonia Society — Rex Begonia Variety Classification
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rex Begonias for Florida Interiors and Shade Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Loving Container Plants for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–June. Polly performs best as a container plant in zone 9, where it can be moved indoors during the rare cold snaps. In the warmest zone 9 areas it may survive as an in-ground perennial in a sheltered, heavily mulched location. Provide very bright indirect light or early morning sun with afternoon shade — direct summer sun bleaches and burns the dramatic dark leaves. Maintain high humidity (mist frequently or place on a humidity tray). In zone 9's warm, humid summers, Polly can be grown outdoors in deep shade for the summer season.

directions: Plant in shade to bright indirect light in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic, consistently moist but well-aerated soil — Alocasia require moisture but are sensitive to root rot in poorly drained or overly wet conditions; African Mask Alocasia (Polly) is a compact cultivar of Alocasia x amazonica, an Aroid hybrid of unknown tropical Southeast Asian species parentage, producing dramatically dark, arrow-shaped to elongated, deeply lobed leaves with prominent, contrasting, chalk-white primary and secondary veins against near-black to deep forest-green leaf surfaces. The high-contrast white vein pattern on the dark leaf — which does indeed have an unsettling resemblance to decorative African masks — is one of the most striking and dramatic foliage effects possible in a houseplant or shade garden specimen. The 'Polly' selection is a more compact form (18–24 inches) than the larger-growing Alocasia x amazonica species, making it more practical for container use and indoor cultivation. Each leaf is simultaneously a botanical and artistic object — the precise geometry of the veining pattern and the glossy, slightly undulating dark green leaf surface create an effect that is more designed than any human art and more dramatic than almost any other commonly grown plant.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alocasia as Landscape and Interior Plants
Missouri Botanical Garden — Alocasia x amazonica Polly
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Plants for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April or September–October. Hello Yellow butterfly weed, like all Asclepias tuberosa, is late to emerge in spring — mark plant locations to avoid disturbing dormant roots. Do not transplant established plants as the deep taproot resists disturbance. Plants bloom in their second year from seed; container-grown plants may bloom the first season. In zone 9 butterfly weed is extremely drought-tolerant once established — one of the most drought-adapted native perennials for full-sun locations.

directions: Plant in full sun in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gritty, neutral to slightly acidic soil — butterfly weed must have excellent drainage and thrives in dry, hot conditions; Hello Yellow is a cultivar of native butterfly weed (Asclepias tuberosa) selected for its clear, bright, warm yellow flower clusters — a color variant of the standard vivid orange species that provides a softer, cooler yellow tone for garden designs where the species orange clashes with surrounding plants. Asclepias tuberosa is one of the most ecologically important native plants for monarch butterfly conservation — it is a host plant for monarch caterpillars and an important nectar source for adult monarchs, swallowtails, and other native pollinators. Unlike the common milkweed (Asclepias syriaca), butterfly weed does not spread aggressively and does not produce the milky latex sap, making it a much more garden-friendly choice that can be integrated into managed landscape plantings without concern for spreading. Hello Yellow's warm yellow flowers pair naturally with orange, bronze, and gold companion plants, creating summer combinations that reference prairie flower meadows where Asclepias tuberosa is naturally found growing alongside goldenrods, coneflowers, and grasses. Drought tolerance, native provenance, and wildlife value make it a cornerstone plant for responsible zone 9 landscapes.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Asclepias tuberosa Native Plant
North American Butterfly Association — Butterfly Weed Host Plant
North Carolina State University Extension — Asclepias tuberosa for Gardens

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm (60°F+); in zone 9 plant March–May. Strawberry Popcorn is harvested for dry kernels rather than fresh eating — leave ears on the stalks until the husks are fully dried and brown, then harvest and continue drying in a warm, well-ventilated location for 3–4 weeks. When the kernels are completely dry, rub ears together to shell the kernels off the cob. The dried ears are ornamental for months before shelling and can be used as fall decorations. Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows (not single long rows) for good wind pollination.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — corn is a heavy feeder requiring nitrogen-rich soil and consistent moisture during ear development; Strawberry Popcorn is an heirloom ornamental and edible popcorn variety producing tiny, round, deep burgundy-crimson ears that are remarkably realistic miniature strawberry look-alikes — the round, plump shape and rich dark red color create an almost uncanny resemblance to a strawberry when the ear is viewed with fresh eyes. The small ears (2–3 inches long) are produced one to two per stalk on compact (4–5 foot) plants, making Strawberry Popcorn both a space-efficient popcorn crop and one of the most attractive ornamental corn varieties for fall decoration. The dark red kernels pop into white popcorn — the kernel color disappears in the heat of popping — producing popcorn with the same light, airy, slightly sweet flavor as standard popcorn. The ornamental appeal (dried ears make excellent fall table decorations, wreaths, and displays) combined with the culinary value (genuinely edible popcorn) makes Strawberry Popcorn one of the best dual-purpose plants for the home garden, particularly appealing to children who delight in both the process of growing them and the surprise of popping the dramatic red kernels into white popcorn.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Strawberry Popcorn Heirloom Corn
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Strawberry Popcorn Description
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Specialty Popcorn Varieties

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–April for fall harvest in August–September. Big Max requires 120–130 days and substantial space — vines spread 8–15 feet. For maximum size, remove all but one or two developing fruits per plant, allowing the plant to concentrate energy into a single massive fruit. Provide a piece of cardboard or wood under developing fruits to prevent rot where they rest on soil. In zone 9 the extreme summer heat can cause pollination failure during the hottest weeks — hand-pollination in early morning may be needed.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, very fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil with ample room for the extensive vines; Big Max is the standard large pumpkin variety for home gardeners seeking impressively large fruits — a Cucurbita maxima (not the common Cucurbita pepo pie/carving pumpkin) that can reach 100 pounds or more under ideal growing conditions, though typical well-grown specimens are 50–80 pounds. The large, round, ribbed, salmon-pink to orange fruits with thick orange flesh are primarily grown for fall display and competition rather than culinary use — the flesh is edible and has a sweeter, less stringy character than standard carving pumpkins, but the enormous size and thick shell make kitchen preparation challenging. The fascination with growing large pumpkins — a pursuit that becomes genuinely competitive at county fairs and pumpkin festivals across North America — has made Big Max and its descendants the gateway variety for the hobby of giant pumpkin growing, which extends through increasingly specialized varieties into fruits exceeding 2,000 pounds. For zone 9 gardeners, Big Max must be started in time to mature before the most intense summer heat — a March planting targets a July–August harvest that avoids both late spring cold and late summer heat extremes.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Large Pumpkin Production
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pumpkin and Gourd Growing
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Pumpkin Production for Gulf South
